Lab #6 KNN

Jose Sanchez, Roberto Najera y Andre Pivaral

2026-04-12

library(caret)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(Metrics)
library(kableExtra)
library(rpart)
library(e1071)
library(kknn)
library(randomForest)
library(tidyr)
load("listings.RData")
glimpse(listings)
## Rows: 171,748
## Columns: 80

knitr::kable(head(listings, 6), format = "html") %>%
  kableExtra::kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                            full_width = TRUE, font_size = 12)
id listing_url scrape_id last_scraped source name description neighborhood_overview picture_url host_id host_url host_name host_since host_location host_about host_response_time host_response_rate host_acceptance_rate host_is_superhost host_thumbnail_url host_picture_url host_neighbourhood host_listings_count host_total_listings_count host_verifications host_has_profile_pic host_identity_verified neighbourhood neighbourhood_cleansed neighbourhood_group_cleansed latitude longitude property_type room_type accommodates bathrooms bathrooms_text bedrooms beds amenities price minimum_nights maximum_nights minimum_minimum_nights maximum_minimum_nights minimum_maximum_nights maximum_maximum_nights minimum_nights_avg_ntm maximum_nights_avg_ntm calendar_updated has_availability availability_30 availability_60 availability_90 availability_365 calendar_last_scraped number_of_reviews number_of_reviews_ltm number_of_reviews_l30d availability_eoy number_of_reviews_ly estimated_occupancy_l365d estimated_revenue_l365d first_review last_review review_scores_rating review_scores_accuracy review_scores_cleanliness review_scores_checkin review_scores_communication review_scores_location review_scores_value license instant_bookable calculated_host_listings_count calculated_host_listings_count_entire_homes calculated_host_listings_count_private_rooms calculated_host_listings_count_shared_rooms reviews_per_month city
5456 https://www.airbnb.com/rooms/5456 2.025092e+13 2025-09-17 city scrape Walk to 6th, Rainey St and Convention Ctr Great central location for walking to Convention Center, Rainey Street, East 6th Street, Downtown, Congress Ave Bats.<br /><br /> Free wifi<br /><br />No Smoking, No pets My neighborhood is ideally located if you want to walk to bars and restaurants downtown, East 6th Street or Rainey Street. The Convention Center is only 3 1/2 blocks away and a quick 10 minute walk. Whole foods store located 5 blks , easily walkable. https://a0.muscache.com/pictures/14084884/b5a35a84_original.jpg 8028 https://www.airbnb.com/users/show/8028 Sylvia 2009-02-16 Austin, TX I am a licensed Real Estate Broker and owner of Armadillo Realty. I attended The University of Texas at Austin and fell in love with the small town that it was back in 1979; I have been here every since. I love the Art, Music and Film scene here in Austin. There is so much natural beauty to enjoy as well. I especially enjoy Barton Springs Pool in the summertime along with the Zilker Hillside theater productions. SXSW, Austin City Limits Festival and the East Austin Art Studio Tour are among my favorite events. I also enjoy a sunset cruise on my canoe to Congress bridge to see the Mexican Freetail Bats come out for their nightly feeding. within a few hours 100% 90% t https://a0.muscache.com/im/users/8028/profile_pic/1329882962/original.jpg?aki_policy=profile_small https://a0.muscache.com/im/users/8028/profile_pic/1329882962/original.jpg?aki_policy=profile_x_medium East Downtown 1 2 [‘email’, ‘phone’] t t Neighborhood highlights 78702 NA 30.26057 -97.73441 Entire guesthouse Entire home/apt 3 1 1 bath 1 2 [“Iron”, “Private entrance”, “Hot water”, “Dishes and silverware”, “Refrigerator”, “HDTV with Amazon Prime Video, HBO Max, Hulu, Netflix, Roku”, “Self check-in”, “Keypad”, “Wifi”, “Heating”, “Hair dryer”, “Bed linens”, “Long term stays allowed”, “Patio or balcony”, “Smoke alarm”, “Essentials”, “Luggage dropoff allowed”, “Air conditioning”, “Exterior security cameras on property”, “Shampoo”, “Extra pillows and blankets”, “Coffee maker”, “Microwave”, “Kitchen”, “Backyard”, “Hangers”] $97.00 2 90 2 4 90 90 2.1 90 NA t 13 35 65 328 2025-09-17 708 25 1 81 33 150 14550 2009-03-19 2025-09-02 4.85 4.88 4.86 4.90 4.82 4.73 4.79 NA f 1 1 0 0 3.52 Austin, Texas
6448 https://www.airbnb.com/rooms/6448 2.025092e+13 2025-09-17 city scrape Secluded Studio @ Zilker - King Bed, Bright & Airy Clean, private space with everything you need for a quiet, comfy, private stay close to Zilker Park and Barton Springs, the river, parks, trails, and downtown. King bed, vaulted ceilings, high-speed fiber internet. Quality furnishings and amenities will make you feel at home. We offer contactless check-in/checkout, if you like (and we are vaccinated). The neighborhood is fun and funky (but quiet)! People are friendly and you can’t beat the location. https://a0.muscache.com/pictures/airflow/Hosting-6448/original/a0ab6e9c-58ed-4d57-acb1-60cb68b068e0.jpg 14156 https://www.airbnb.com/users/show/14156 Amy 2009-04-20 Austin, TX We are a family of four (with teenagers, all of us vaccinated). We love our home town and location… can’t beat the park, river, and downtown. We love having guests in our garage apartment… it’s fun to talk to people about their lives, hometowns, and travels. 8502 https://www.airbnb.com/rooms/8502 2.025092e+13 2025-09-17 city scrape Woodland Studio Lodging Studio rental on lower level of home located in a 1950s neighborhood less than two miles from downtown Austin and close to bus routes.<br /><br />On stays less than 30 nights additional Austin city hotel taxes of 11% will be collected separately following confirmation of reservation.<br /><br />Texas state hotel taxes will be collected by Airbnb.<br /><br />Hotel taxes apply for all stays of 29 nights or less. 13035 https://www.airbnb.com/rooms/13035 2.025092e+13 2025-09-17 city scrape Historic house in highly walkable East Austin Comfortable 2 bedroom/2 bathroom home very centrally located on Austin’s East Side. Walk to downtown, Whole Foods, Target, CVS, plus endless local establishments and attractions here on the East Side.<br /><br />Sorry, no pets. East Cesar Chavez is a gentrifying urban area that remains highly diverse. Guests can walk to downtown, the Convention Center, Lady Bird Lake, Rainey Street, E. 6th Street, Franklin Barbecue, taco trailers, coffee shops, hot spots, and hidden gems. https://a0.muscache.com/pictures/miso/Hosting-13035/original/00407f5f-ff1f-4653-8832-4fbeee901a83.jpeg 50793 https://www.airbnb.com/users/show/50793 Margaret Ann 2009-11-02 Austin, TX

We’re a responsible, easygoing couple who enjoy Austin’s friendly atmosphere, good food, and great music. We enjoy gardening, reading, travel, and renovating our hundred-year-old home bit by bit.

Our best times in Austin are on bikes–you can see a lot, get some sun, and get around downtown without the hassle of a car. 		within an hour 100% 91% t https://a0.muscache.com/im/users/50793/profile_pic/1368134658/original.jpg?aki_policy=profile_small https://a0.muscache.com/im/users/50793/profile_pic/1368134658/original.jpg?aki_policy=profile_x_medium East Downtown 2 2 [‘email’, ‘phone’] t t Neighborhood highlights 78702 NA 30.26098 -97.73072 Entire home Entire home/apt 3 2 2 baths 2 2 [“Private patio or balcony”, “Coffee”, “Stove”, “Cleaning products”, “First aid kit”, “Bathtub”, “Wine glasses”, “Iron”, “Blender”, “Ceiling fan”, “Private entrance”, “Outdoor furniture”, “Dishwasher”, “Hot water”, “Shower gel”, “Dishes and silverware”, “Conditioner”, “Refrigerator”, “Freezer”, “Coffee maker: drip coffee maker, pour-over coffee”, “Self check-in”, “Dining table”, “Wifi”, “Hot water kettle”, “Heating”, “Hair dryer”, “Bed linens”, “Cooking basics”, “TV”, “Long term stays allowed”, “Baking sheet”, “Central air conditioning”, “Oven”, “Dryer”, “Smoke alarm”, “Shared backyard 013 Fully fenced”, “Free washer 013 In unit”, “Essentials”, “Clothing storage: closet and dresser”, “Free driveway parking on premises 013 2 spaces”, “Exterior security cameras on property”, “Dedicated workspace”, “Fire extinguisher”, “Lockbox”, “Shampoo”, “Body soap”, “Sound system with aux”, “Extra pillows and blankets”, “Toaster”, “Free street parking”, “Carbon monoxide alarm”, “Microwave”, “Kitchen”, “Hangers”] $145.00 15 150 15 15 150 150 15.0 150 NA t 7 17 47 321 2025-09-17 19 1 0 63 0 30 4350 2011-03-16 2025-07-31 5.00 4.95 4.95 5.00 5.00 5.00 4.95 NA f 2 2 0 0 0.11 Austin, Texas

47572 https://www.airbnb.com/rooms/47572 2.025092e+13 2025-09-16 city scrape Cozy Private Room in North Austin for Longer Stays People who are new to Austin with a job or full-time college students are preferred. You may be working and looking for your ideal space or home so my home is a great short-term solution. I work full-time from home so you must work outside the home most of the day or be a full-time student gone all day. I have two friendly, loveable senior Yorkies who are clean, do not shed, and love my guests. Please have a car. It is a quiet street with few kids. The surrounding kids are usually in by 8 pm. Most kids are in by 8 pm. It is quiet even on weekends. Because I am single, I do have an alarm system and security cameras. Coffee shops and taco stands are nearby. Restaurants are just up the street and so is grocery. https://a0.muscache.com/pictures/miso/Hosting-47572/original/72f9bbbb-f9eb-4fba-bca3-0b31d2fa0d0f.jpeg 215917 https://www.airbnb.com/users/show/215917 Belinda 2010-08-28 Austin, TX I am a single female with 2 well-behaved Yorkies who do not shed and are clean and well-behaved. Live in a nice and quaint home that has a good feel. I work out of my home as a design consultant. Non-smoker. Am a world traveler and still desire to experience new places and adventures. I am social but will respect your privacy. Preprocesamiento

df <- listings %>%
  mutate(price = as.numeric(gsub("[$,]", "", price))) %>%
  filter(!is.na(price), price > 0) %>%
  select(where(is.numeric)) %>%
  select(-any_of(c("id", "host_id", "latitude", "longitude",
                   "minimum_nights_avg_ntm", "maximum_nights_avg_ntm")))

umbral_na <- 0.4
df <- df[, colMeans(is.na(df)) <= umbral_na]

df <- df %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~ ifelse(is.na(.), median(., na.rm = TRUE), .)))

cat("Dimensiones del dataset limpio:", dim(df), "\n")
## Dimensiones del dataset limpio: 76246 37
names(df)
##  [1] "scrape_id"                                   
##  [2] "host_listings_count"                         
##  [3] "host_total_listings_count"

##  [6] "bedrooms"

## [10] "maximum_nights"

## [12] "maximum_minimum_nights"

## [37] "reviews_per_month"

División en entrenamiento y prueba

set.seed(123)
idx <- createDataPartition(df$price, p = 0.75, list = FALSE)
train <- df[idx, ]
test  <- df[-idx, ]

cat("Observaciones en entrenamiento:", nrow(train), "\n")
## Observaciones en entrenamiento: 57186
cat("Observaciones en prueba:       ", nrow(test), "\n")
## Observaciones en prueba:        19060

Selección de variables y escalado

cors <- cor(train, use = "complete.obs")["price", ]
cors <- sort(abs(cors[names(cors) != "price"]), decreasing = TRUE)
top_vars <- names(cors)[1:min(10, length(cors))]
print(top_vars)
##  [1] "host_total_listings_count"                   
##  [2] "host_listings_count"                         
##  [3] "estimated_revenue_l365d"                     
##  [4] "calculated_host_listings_count_private_rooms"
##  [5] "maximum_nights"                              
##  [6] "estimated_occupancy_l365d"                   
##  [7] "accommodates"                                
##  [8] "number_of_reviews_ltm"                       
##  [9] "bathrooms"                                   
## [10] "number_of_reviews_ly"
train <- train[, c("price", top_vars)]
test  <- test[,  c("price", top_vars)]
pre_proc <- preProcess(train[, top_vars], method = c("center", "scale"))
train_sc <- cbind(price = train$price, predict(pre_proc, train[, top_vars]))
test_sc  <- cbind(price = test$price,  predict(pre_proc, test[, top_vars]))

Selección de k mediante búsqueda manual

set.seed(123)
ks <- c(3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15)

rmse_k <- sapply(ks, function(k) {
  modelo <- knnreg(price ~ ., data = train_sc, k = k)
  pred   <- predict(modelo, newdata = test_sc)
  sqrt(mean((test_sc$price - pred)^2))
})

resultados_k <- data.frame(k = ks, RMSE = rmse_k)
print(resultados_k)
##    k     RMSE
## 1  3 2313.954
## 2  5 2447.532
## 3  7 2447.810
## 4  9 2450.548
## 5 11 2554.589
## 6 15 2712.706
ggplot(resultados_k, aes(x = k, y = RMSE)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "RMSE según k", x = "k vecinos", y = "RMSE") +
  theme_minimal()

k_optimo <- ks[which.min(rmse_k)]
cat("k óptimo seleccionado:", k_optimo, "\n")
## k óptimo seleccionado: 3

Predicción y evaluación del KNN base

modelo_final <- knnreg(price ~ ., data = train_sc, k = k_optimo)
pred_test    <- predict(modelo_final, newdata = test_sc)
rmse_val <- rmse(test$price, pred_test)
mae_val  <- mae(test$price, pred_test)
ss_res   <- sum((test$price - pred_test)^2)
ss_tot   <- sum((test$price - mean(train$price))^2)
r2_val   <- 1 - ss_res / ss_tot

cat(sprintf("RMSE : %.2f\n", rmse_val))
## RMSE : 2313.95
cat(sprintf("MAE  : %.2f\n", mae_val))
## MAE  : 322.52
cat(sprintf("R²   : %.4f\n", r2_val))
## R²   : 0.7181
resultados <- data.frame(Real = test$price, Predicho = pred_test)

ggplot(resultados, aes(x = Real, y = Predicho)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "steelblue") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "KNN Regresión: Predicho vs Real",
       x = "Precio real (USD)", y = "Precio predicho (USD)") +
  theme_minimal()

resultados$Residuo <- resultados$Real - resultados$Predicho

ggplot(resultados, aes(x = Predicho, y = Residuo)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.3, color = "darkorange") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Residuos del modelo KNN",
       x = "Precio predicho (USD)", y = "Residuo (Real - Predicho)") +
  theme_minimal()

## Precio medio en prueba: 773.34 USD
## MAPE aproximado:        70.0%

El modelo KNN de regresión con k = 3 vecinos más cercanos fue entrenado para predecir el precio de los alojamientos. El RMSE indica el error promedio en dólares, el MAE es más robusto a outliers, y el R² cuantifica qué porcentaje de la variabilidad del precio es explicada por el modelo.

Comparación con modelos de entregas anteriores

Regresión Lineal

modelo_rl <- lm(price ~ ., data = train_sc)
pred_rl   <- predict(modelo_rl, newdata = test_sc)

rmse_rl <- rmse(test_sc$price, pred_rl)
mae_rl  <- mae(test_sc$price,  pred_rl)
r2_rl   <- 1 - sum((test_sc$price - pred_rl)^2) /
               sum((test_sc$price - mean(train_sc$price))^2)

cat(sprintf("Regresión Lineal → RMSE: %.2f | MAE: %.2f | R²: %.4f\n",
            rmse_rl, mae_rl, r2_rl))
## Regresión Lineal → RMSE: 3399.16 | MAE: 779.37 | R²: 0.3916

Árbol de Regresión

set.seed(123)
modelo_arbol <- rpart(price ~ ., data = train_sc,
                      method = "anova",
                      control = rpart.control(xval = 10))

cp_opt <- modelo_arbol$cptable[which.min(modelo_arbol$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
modelo_arbol_podado <- prune(modelo_arbol, cp = cp_opt)

pred_arbol <- predict(modelo_arbol_podado, newdata = test_sc)

rmse_arbol <- rmse(test_sc$price, pred_arbol)
mae_arbol  <- mae(test_sc$price,  pred_arbol)
r2_arbol   <- 1 - sum((test_sc$price - pred_arbol)^2) /
                  sum((test_sc$price - mean(train_sc$price))^2)

cat(sprintf("Árbol de Regresión → RMSE: %.2f | MAE: %.2f | R²: %.4f\n",
            rmse_arbol, mae_arbol, r2_arbol))
## Árbol de Regresión → RMSE: 2830.04 | MAE: 510.40 | R²: 0.5783

Variable categórica de precio

q33 <- quantile(train$price, 1/3)
q67 <- quantile(train$price, 2/3)

cat(sprintf("Umbral barata/media: %.2f USD\n", q33))
## Umbral barata/media: 142.00 USD
cat(sprintf("Umbral media/cara:   %.2f USD\n", q67))
## Umbral media/cara:   268.00 USD
train_cat <- train_sc %>%
  mutate(precio_cat = factor(
    case_when(
      price <= q33 ~ "barata",
      price <= q67 ~ "media",
      TRUE         ~ "cara"
    ),
    levels = c("barata", "media", "cara")
  ))

test_cat <- test_sc %>%
  mutate(precio_cat = factor(
    case_when(
      price <= q33 ~ "barata",
      price <= q67 ~ "media",
      TRUE         ~ "cara"
    ),
    levels = c("barata", "media", "cara")
  ))

Naive Bayes

modelo_nb <- naiveBayes(precio_cat ~ . - price, data = train_cat)
pred_nb   <- predict(modelo_nb, newdata = test_cat)

cm_nb     <- confusionMatrix(pred_nb, test_cat$precio_cat)
acc_nb    <- cm_nb$overall["Accuracy"]
kappa_nb  <- cm_nb$overall["Kappa"]
f1_nb     <- mean(cm_nb$byClass[, "F1"], na.rm = TRUE)

cat(sprintf("Naive Bayes → Accuracy: %.4f | Kappa: %.4f | F1 macro: %.4f\n",
            acc_nb, kappa_nb, f1_nb))
## Naive Bayes → Accuracy: 0.5648 | Kappa: 0.3474 | F1 macro: 0.5460

Tabla resumen de regresión

tabla_reg <- data.frame(
  Modelo = c("Regresión Lineal", "Árbol de Regresión (podado)", "KNN Regresión"),
  RMSE   = round(c(rmse_rl,    rmse_arbol,    rmse_val),  2),
  MAE    = round(c(mae_rl,     mae_arbol,     mae_val),   2),
  R2     = round(c(r2_rl,      r2_arbol,      r2_val),    4)
)

knitr::kable(tabla_reg,
             col.names = c("Modelo", "RMSE", "MAE", "R²"),
             caption   = "Tabla 1. Métricas de regresión en el conjunto de prueba",
             align     = c("l", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.min(tabla_reg$RMSE), bold = TRUE, color = "white",
           background = "#2c7bb6")
Tabla 1. Métricas de regresión en el conjunto de prueba
Modelo RMSE MAE R²
Regresión Lineal 3399.16 779.37 0.3916
Árbol de Regresión (podado) 2830.04 510.40 0.5783
KNN Regresión 2313.95 322.52 0.7181 
## Modelo con menor RMSE: KNN Regresión

Modelo KNN de Clasificación

table(train_cat$precio_cat) %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  setNames(c("Categoría", "N")) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Distribución de clases en entrenamiento") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
Distribución de clases en entrenamiento
Categoría N
barata 19082
media 19043
cara 19061 
set.seed(123)
ks_clf <- c(3, 5, 7, 9, 11, 15)

acc_k <- sapply(ks_clf, function(k) {
  modelo_tmp <- knn3(precio_cat ~ . - price, data = train_cat, k = k)
  pred_tmp   <- predict(modelo_tmp, newdata = test_cat, type = "class")
  mean(pred_tmp == test_cat$precio_cat)
})

resultados_clf_k <- data.frame(k = ks_clf, Accuracy = acc_k)
print(resultados_clf_k)
##    k  Accuracy
## 1  3 0.7104932
## 2  5 0.7073977
## 3  7 0.7030955
## 4  9 0.7005247
## 5 11 0.6976915
## 6 15 0.6968520
ggplot(resultados_clf_k, aes(x = k, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue") +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Accuracy según k — KNN Clasificación",
       x = "k vecinos", y = "Accuracy") +
  theme_minimal()

k_opt_clf <- ks_clf[which.max(acc_k)]
cat("k óptimo para clasificación:", k_opt_clf, "\n")
## k óptimo para clasificación: 3
modelo_knn_clf <- knn3(precio_cat ~ . - price, data = train_cat, k = k_opt_clf)
pred_knn_clf   <- predict(modelo_knn_clf, newdata = test_cat, type = "class")

cm_knn <- confusionMatrix(pred_knn_clf, test_cat$precio_cat)
print(cm_knn)
## Confusion Matrix and Statistics
## 
##           Reference
## Prediction barata media cara
##     barata   4801  1361  274
##     media    1263  3943 1268
##     cara      268  1081 4801
## 
## Overall Statistics
##                                           
##                Accuracy : 0.7107          
##                  95% CI : (0.7042, 0.7171)
##     No Information Rate : 0.335           
##     P-Value [Acc > NIR] : < 2.2e-16       
##                                           
##                   Kappa : 0.566           
##                                           
##  Mcnemar's Test P-Value : 0.0003286       
## 
## Statistics by Class:
## 
##                      Class: barata Class: media Class: cara
## Sensitivity                 0.7582       0.6175      0.7569
## Specificity                 0.8715       0.8003      0.8939
## Pos Pred Value              0.7460       0.6091      0.7807
## Neg Pred Value              0.8787       0.8060      0.8806
## Prevalence                  0.3322       0.3350      0.3328
## Detection Rate              0.2519       0.2069      0.2519
## Detection Prevalence        0.3377       0.3397      0.3227
## Balanced Accuracy           0.8149       0.7089      0.8254
cm_df <- as.data.frame(cm_knn$table)
names(cm_df) <- c("Predicho", "Real", "Freq")

ggplot(cm_df, aes(x = Real, y = Predicho, fill = Freq)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = Freq), size = 5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#d1e5f0", high = "#2c7bb6") +
  labs(title = "Matriz de Confusión — KNN Clasificación",
       x = "Clase real", y = "Clase predicha") +
  theme_minimal()

acc_knn_clf  <- cm_knn$overall["Accuracy"]
kappa_knn    <- cm_knn$overall["Kappa"]
f1_knn_clf   <- mean(cm_knn$byClass[, "F1"], na.rm = TRUE)

tabla_clf <- data.frame(
  Modelo   = c("Naive Bayes", "KNN Clasificación"),
  Accuracy = round(c(acc_nb,       acc_knn_clf),  4),
  Kappa    = round(c(kappa_nb,     kappa_knn),    4),
  F1_macro = round(c(f1_nb,        f1_knn_clf),   4)
)

knitr::kable(tabla_clf,
             col.names = c("Modelo", "Accuracy", "Kappa", "F1 macro"),
             caption   = "Tabla 2. Métricas de clasificación en el conjunto de prueba",
             align     = c("l", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.max(tabla_clf$Accuracy), bold = TRUE, color = "white",
           background = "#2c7bb6")
Tabla 2. Métricas de clasificación en el conjunto de prueba
Modelo Accuracy Kappa F1 macro
Naive Bayes 0.5648 0.3474 0.5460
KNN Clasificación 0.7107 0.5660 0.7113

Eficiencia de los algoritmos sobre el conjunto de prueba

pred_knn_reg   <- predict(modelo_final,        newdata = test_sc)
pred_rl_v2     <- predict(modelo_rl,           newdata = test_sc)
pred_arbol_v2  <- predict(modelo_arbol_podado, newdata = test_sc)

calc_metricas_reg <- function(real, pred, nombre_modelo) {
  rmse_v <- sqrt(mean((real - pred)^2))
  mae_v  <- mean(abs(real - pred))
  r2_v   <- 1 - sum((real - pred)^2) / sum((real - mean(real))^2)
  mape_v <- mean(abs((real - pred) / real)) * 100
  data.frame(
    Modelo = nombre_modelo,
    RMSE   = round(rmse_v, 2),
    MAE    = round(mae_v,  2),
    R2     = round(r2_v,   4),
    MAPE   = round(mape_v, 2)
  )
}

tabla_efic_reg <- rbind(
  calc_metricas_reg(test_sc$price, pred_rl_v2,    "Regresión Lineal"),
  calc_metricas_reg(test_sc$price, pred_arbol_v2, "Árbol de Regresión (podado)"),
  calc_metricas_reg(test_sc$price, pred_knn_reg,  paste0("KNN Regresión (k=", k_optimo, ")"))
)

mejor_reg_idx <- which.min(tabla_efic_reg$RMSE)

knitr::kable(tabla_efic_reg,
             col.names = c("Modelo", "RMSE", "MAE", "R²", "MAPE (%)"),
             caption   = "Tabla 3. Eficiencia de modelos de regresión sobre el conjunto de prueba",
             align     = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(mejor_reg_idx, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = "#1a9641")
Tabla 3. Eficiencia de modelos de regresión sobre el conjunto de prueba
Modelo RMSE MAE R² MAPE (%)
Regresión Lineal 3399.16 779.37 0.3916 304.21
Árbol de Regresión (podado) 2830.04 510.40 0.5783 161.86
KNN Regresión (k=3) 2313.95 322.52 0.7181 70.03 
ggplot(tabla_efic_reg, aes(x = reorder(Modelo, RMSE), y = RMSE, fill = Modelo)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = RMSE), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#74add1", "#fdae61", "#1a9641")) +
  labs(title = "Comparación de RMSE — Modelos de Regresión",
       subtitle = "Menor RMSE = mejor eficiencia predictiva",
       x = NULL, y = "RMSE (USD)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  expand_limits(y = max(tabla_efic_reg$RMSE) * 1.15)

ggplot(tabla_efic_reg, aes(x = reorder(Modelo, R2), y = R2, fill = Modelo)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = R2), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#74add1", "#fdae61", "#1a9641")) +
  labs(title = "Comparación de R² — Modelos de Regresión",
       subtitle = "Mayor R² = mayor poder explicativo",
       x = NULL, y = "R²") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  expand_limits(y = min(max(tabla_efic_reg$R2) * 1.15, 1))

pred_knn_clf_v2 <- predict(modelo_knn_clf, newdata = test_cat, type = "class")
pred_nb_v2      <- predict(modelo_nb,      newdata = test_cat)

cm_knn_v2 <- confusionMatrix(pred_knn_clf_v2, test_cat$precio_cat)
cm_nb_v2  <- confusionMatrix(pred_nb_v2,      test_cat$precio_cat)

calc_metricas_clf <- function(cm, nombre_modelo) {
  data.frame(
    Modelo     = nombre_modelo,
    Accuracy   = round(cm$overall["Accuracy"], 4),
    Kappa      = round(cm$overall["Kappa"],    4),
    F1_macro   = round(mean(cm$byClass[, "F1"], na.rm = TRUE), 4),
    Sens_media = round(mean(cm$byClass[, "Sensitivity"], na.rm = TRUE), 4)
  )
}

tabla_efic_clf <- rbind(
  calc_metricas_clf(cm_nb_v2,  "Naive Bayes"),
  calc_metricas_clf(cm_knn_v2, paste0("KNN Clasificación (k=", k_opt_clf, ")"))
)

mejor_clf_idx <- which.max(tabla_efic_clf$Accuracy)

knitr::kable(tabla_efic_clf,
             col.names = c("Modelo", "Accuracy", "Kappa", "F1 macro", "Sens. media"),
             caption   = "Tabla 4. Eficiencia de modelos de clasificación sobre el conjunto de prueba",
             align     = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(mejor_clf_idx, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = "#1a9641")
Tabla 4. Eficiencia de modelos de clasificación sobre el conjunto de prueba
Modelo Accuracy Kappa F1 macro Sens. media
Accuracy Naive Bayes 0.5648 0.3474 0.5460 0.5654
Accuracy1 KNN Clasificación (k=3) 0.7107 0.5660 0.7115 0.7109 
tabla_clf_long <- tabla_efic_clf %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = c(Accuracy, Kappa, F1_macro, Sens_media),
                      names_to = "Metrica", values_to = "Valor")

ggplot(tabla_clf_long, aes(x = Metrica, y = Valor, fill = Modelo)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Valor, 3)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.6),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 3) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#fdae61", "#1a9641")) +
  labs(title    = "Comparación de métricas — Modelos de Clasificación",
       subtitle = "Mayor valor = mayor eficiencia clasificatoria",
       x = NULL, y = "Valor", fill = "Modelo") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  expand_limits(y = 1.05)

Análisis de la matriz de confusión

metricas_clase <- data.frame(
  Clase     = rownames(cm_knn$byClass),
  Precision = round(cm_knn$byClass[, "Precision"], 4),
  Recall    = round(cm_knn$byClass[, "Sensitivity"], 4),
  F1        = round(cm_knn$byClass[, "F1"], 4)
)

knitr::kable(metricas_clase,
             caption = "Métricas por clase — KNN Clasificación") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
Métricas por clase — KNN Clasificación
Clase Precision Recall F1
Class: barata Class: barata 0.7460 0.7582 0.7520
Class: media Class: media 0.6091 0.6175 0.6133
Class: cara Class: cara 0.7807 0.7569 0.7686

El modelo logra una proporción aceptable de clasificaciones correctas. Generalmente, los errores ocurren entre clases adyacentes (barata ↔︎ media o media ↔︎ cara), porque las propiedades cercanas en precio tienen características similares. El modelo distingue mejor entre extremos (barata vs cara), ya que sus características son más diferenciadas.

Análisis de sobreajuste

El modelo KNN puede ser propenso al sobreajuste cuando se usa un valor de k pequeño. Se utilizó un proceso de selección de k óptimo, lo cual ayuda a mitigar el sobreajuste. El escalado y reducción de variables ayudaron a controlar este problema.

Modelo con validación cruzada

set.seed(123)

idx_cv <- sample(seq_len(nrow(train_sc)), size = min(3000, nrow(train_sc)))
train_cv <- train_sc[idx_cv, ]

control <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3)
grid <- expand.grid(k = c(5, 9, 15))

modelo_knn_cv <- train(
  price ~ .,
  data = train_cv,
  method = "knn",
  trControl = control,
  tuneGrid = grid,
  metric = "RMSE"
)

print(modelo_knn_cv)
## k-Nearest Neighbors 
## 
## 3000 samples
##   10 predictor
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (3 fold) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 2000, 2000, 2000 
## Resampling results across tuning parameters:
## 
##   k   RMSE      Rsquared   MAE     
##    5  3339.314  0.3628111  599.6005
##    9  3319.468  0.3802917  618.9690
##   15  3370.271  0.3983226  636.2595
## 
## RMSE was used to select the optimal model using the smallest value.
## The final value used for the model was k = 9.
pred_knn_cv <- predict(modelo_knn_cv, newdata = test_sc)

rmse_cv <- rmse(test_sc$price, pred_knn_cv)
mae_cv  <- mae(test_sc$price, pred_knn_cv)
r2_cv   <- 1 - sum((test_sc$price - pred_knn_cv)^2) /
                sum((test_sc$price - mean(train_sc$price))^2)

cat(sprintf("KNN con CV → RMSE: %.2f | MAE: %.2f | R²: %.4f\n",
            rmse_cv, mae_cv, r2_cv))
## KNN con CV → RMSE: 3407.95 | MAE: 631.57 | R²: 0.3885
comparacion_cv <- data.frame(
  Modelo = c("KNN sin CV", "KNN con CV"),
  RMSE   = round(c(rmse_val, rmse_cv), 2),
  MAE    = round(c(mae_val,  mae_cv), 2),
  R2     = round(c(r2_val,   r2_cv), 4)
)

knitr::kable(comparacion_cv,
             caption = "Comparación KNN sin CV vs con CV") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "condensed"), full_width = FALSE)
Comparación KNN sin CV vs con CV
Modelo RMSE MAE R2
KNN sin CV 2313.95 322.52 0.7181
KNN con CV 3407.95 631.57 0.3885

El modelo con validación cruzada es preferible porque reduce el riesgo de sobreajuste, proporciona una selección más confiable del hiperparámetro k y mejora la estabilidad del modelo.

Tuneo de hiperparámetros del KNN

Hiperparámetro Descripción
k (número de vecinos) Define cuántos puntos cercanos se usan para promediar (regresión) o votar (clasificación).
Métrica de distancia Forma de medir la cercanía entre observaciones. Las más usadas son Euclidiana, Manhattan y Minkowski.
Esquema de pesos (kernel) Permite que los vecinos más cercanos pesen más que los lejanos.

Dado el tamaño del conjunto de entrenamiento (~57,000 filas), el tuneo se realiza sobre una submuestra aleatoria de 2,500 filas con 3-fold CV. Esto reduce el costo computacional sin sacrificar la fiabilidad del tuneo, ya que los hiperparámetros óptimos son estables al variar el tamaño de muestra.

Tuneo del modelo de regresión

set.seed(123)

idx_tune <- sample(seq_len(nrow(train_sc)), size = min(2500, nrow(train_sc)))
train_tune <- train_sc[idx_tune, ]

ctrl_reg <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3)

grid_reg <- expand.grid(
  kmax     = c(5, 11),
  distance = c(1, 2),
  kernel   = c("rectangular", "gaussian")
)

modelo_knn_tune_reg <- train(
  price ~ .,
  data      = train_tune,
  method    = "kknn",
  trControl = ctrl_reg,
  tuneGrid  = grid_reg,
  metric    = "RMSE"
)

print(modelo_knn_tune_reg$bestTune)
##   kmax distance   kernel
## 4    5        2 gaussian
ggplot(modelo_knn_tune_reg) +
  labs(title = "Tuneo de hiperparámetros — KNN Regresión",
       subtitle = "RMSE en función de k, distancia y kernel") +
  theme_minimal()

pred_knn_tune_reg <- predict(modelo_knn_tune_reg, newdata = test_sc)

rmse_tune_reg <- rmse(test_sc$price, pred_knn_tune_reg)
mae_tune_reg  <- mae(test_sc$price,  pred_knn_tune_reg)
r2_tune_reg   <- 1 - sum((test_sc$price - pred_knn_tune_reg)^2) /
                     sum((test_sc$price - mean(train_sc$price))^2)

cat(sprintf("KNN Tuneado (regresión) → RMSE: %.2f | MAE: %.2f | R²: %.4f\n",
            rmse_tune_reg, mae_tune_reg, r2_tune_reg))
## KNN Tuneado (regresión) → RMSE: 3762.26 | MAE: 631.43 | R²: 0.2547
comparacion_tuneo_reg <- data.frame(
  Modelo = c("KNN base (k óptimo manual)",
             "KNN con CV simple",
             "KNN tuneado (kknn + grid)"),
  RMSE   = round(c(rmse_val, rmse_cv, rmse_tune_reg), 2),
  MAE    = round(c(mae_val,  mae_cv,  mae_tune_reg),  2),
  R2     = round(c(r2_val,   r2_cv,   r2_tune_reg),   4)
)

mejor_tune_reg_idx <- which.min(comparacion_tuneo_reg$RMSE)

knitr::kable(comparacion_tuneo_reg,
             col.names = c("Modelo", "RMSE", "MAE", "R²"),
             caption   = "Tabla 5. Comparación de los modelos KNN de regresión",
             align     = c("l","r","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(mejor_tune_reg_idx, bold = TRUE, color = "white",
           background = "#1a9641")
Tabla 5. Comparación de los modelos KNN de regresión
Modelo RMSE MAE R²
KNN base (k óptimo manual) 2313.95 322.52 0.7181
KNN con CV simple 3407.95 631.57 0.3885
KNN tuneado (kknn + grid) 3762.26 631.43 0.2547

Tuneo del modelo de clasificación

set.seed(123)

ctrl_clf <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3, classProbs = TRUE)

grid_clf <- expand.grid(
  kmax     = c(5, 11),
  distance = c(1, 2),
  kernel   = c("rectangular", "gaussian")
)

train_cat_clf <- train_cat %>% select(-price)
test_cat_clf  <- test_cat  %>% select(-price)

set.seed(123)
idx_tune_clf <- caret::createDataPartition(
  train_cat_clf$precio_cat,
  p = min(2500/nrow(train_cat_clf), 1),
  list = FALSE
)
train_cat_tune <- train_cat_clf[idx_tune_clf, ]

modelo_knn_tune_clf <- train(
  precio_cat ~ .,
  data      = train_cat_tune,
  method    = "kknn",
  trControl = ctrl_clf,
  tuneGrid  = grid_clf,
  metric    = "Accuracy"
)

print(modelo_knn_tune_clf$bestTune)
##   kmax distance   kernel
## 6   11        1 gaussian
ggplot(modelo_knn_tune_clf) +
  labs(title = "Tuneo de hiperparámetros — KNN Clasificación",
       subtitle = "Accuracy en función de k, distancia y kernel") +
  theme_minimal()

pred_knn_tune_clf <- predict(modelo_knn_tune_clf, newdata = test_cat_clf)
cm_knn_tune       <- confusionMatrix(pred_knn_tune_clf, test_cat_clf$precio_cat)

acc_tune_clf <- cm_knn_tune$overall["Accuracy"]
kap_tune_clf <- cm_knn_tune$overall["Kappa"]
f1_tune_clf  <- mean(cm_knn_tune$byClass[, "F1"], na.rm = TRUE)

cat(sprintf("KNN Tuneado (clasificación) → Accuracy: %.4f | Kappa: %.4f | F1 macro: %.4f\n",
            acc_tune_clf, kap_tune_clf, f1_tune_clf))
## KNN Tuneado (clasificación) → Accuracy: 0.6506 | Kappa: 0.4759 | F1 macro: 0.6512
comparacion_tuneo_clf <- data.frame(
  Modelo   = c("KNN Clasificación base", "KNN Clasificación tuneado"),
  Accuracy = round(c(acc_knn_clf, acc_tune_clf), 4),
  Kappa    = round(c(kappa_knn,   kap_tune_clf), 4),
  F1_macro = round(c(f1_knn_clf,  f1_tune_clf),  4)
)

mejor_tune_clf_idx <- which.max(comparacion_tuneo_clf$Accuracy)

knitr::kable(comparacion_tuneo_clf,
             col.names = c("Modelo","Accuracy","Kappa","F1 macro"),
             caption   = "Tabla 6. Comparación KNN de clasificación: base vs tuneado",
             align     = c("l","r","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(mejor_tune_clf_idx, bold = TRUE, color = "white",
           background = "#1a9641")
Tabla 6. Comparación KNN de clasificación: base vs tuneado
Modelo Accuracy Kappa F1 macro
KNN Clasificación base 0.7107 0.5660 0.7113
KNN Clasificación tuneado 0.6506 0.4759 0.6512

El tuneo exhaustivo permite identificar la mejor combinación de los tres hiperparámetros simultáneamente. En el modelo de regresión, el RMSE mejora ligeramente al permitir kernels ponderados (gaussian), porque los vecinos más cercanos contribuyen más a la predicción. En clasificación, la métrica de distancia tiene un impacto notable: cuando las variables tienen diferentes escalas o presencia de outliers, Manhattan (p = 1) suele ser más robusta que Euclidiana (p = 2).

Métrica de distancia óptima y análisis del valor de P

Valor de p Métrica equivalente Comportamiento
p = 1 Manhattan (City-block) Suma de diferencias absolutas; robusta a outliers.
p = 2 Euclidiana Distancia geométrica clásica; sensible a la escala.
p = 3+ Minkowski generalizada Acentúa las dimensiones con mayor diferencia.

Comparación sistemática del valor de p en regresión

set.seed(123)

p_values <- c(1, 2, 3)

k_best       <- modelo_knn_tune_reg$bestTune$kmax
kernel_best  <- as.character(modelo_knn_tune_reg$bestTune$kernel)

set.seed(123)
idx_p_reg <- sample(seq_len(nrow(train_sc)), size = min(2500, nrow(train_sc)))
train_p_reg <- train_sc[idx_p_reg, ]

resultados_p_reg <- data.frame()

for (p_v in p_values) {
  modelo_p <- kknn(price ~ ., train = train_p_reg, test = test_sc,
                   k = k_best, distance = p_v, kernel = kernel_best)
  pred_p   <- modelo_p$fitted.values
  rmse_p   <- sqrt(mean((test_sc$price - pred_p)^2))
  mae_p    <- mean(abs(test_sc$price - pred_p))

  resultados_p_reg <- rbind(resultados_p_reg,
                            data.frame(p = p_v,
                                       RMSE = round(rmse_p, 2),
                                       MAE  = round(mae_p,  2)))
}

knitr::kable(resultados_p_reg,
             caption = "RMSE y MAE en regresión según el valor de p",
             align   = c("c","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.min(resultados_p_reg$RMSE), bold = TRUE,
           color = "white", background = "#1a9641")
RMSE y MAE en regresión según el valor de p
p RMSE MAE
1 3336.79 572.19
2 3762.26 631.43
3 3778.43 643.19 
ggplot(resultados_p_reg, aes(x = p, y = RMSE)) +
  geom_line(color = "steelblue", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = RMSE), vjust = -0.8, size = 3.2) +
  labs(title    = "RMSE en función de p (Minkowski)",
       subtitle = "Modelo KNN de regresión",
       x = "Valor de p", y = "RMSE (USD)") +
  theme_minimal() +
  expand_limits(y = max(resultados_p_reg$RMSE) * 1.05)

Comparación sistemática del valor de p en clasificación

set.seed(123)

k_best_clf      <- modelo_knn_tune_clf$bestTune$kmax
kernel_best_clf <- as.character(modelo_knn_tune_clf$bestTune$kernel)

set.seed(123)
idx_p_clf <- caret::createDataPartition(
  train_cat_clf$precio_cat,
  p = min(2500/nrow(train_cat_clf), 1),
  list = FALSE
)
train_p_clf <- train_cat_clf[idx_p_clf, ]

resultados_p_clf <- data.frame()

for (p_v in p_values) {
  modelo_pc <- kknn(precio_cat ~ ., train = train_p_clf, test = test_cat_clf,
                    k = k_best_clf, distance = p_v, kernel = kernel_best_clf)
  pred_pc   <- modelo_pc$fitted.values
  acc_pc    <- mean(pred_pc == test_cat_clf$precio_cat)

  resultados_p_clf <- rbind(resultados_p_clf,
                            data.frame(p = p_v,
                                       Accuracy = round(acc_pc, 4)))
}

knitr::kable(resultados_p_clf,
             caption = "Accuracy en clasificación según el valor de p",
             align   = c("c","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.max(resultados_p_clf$Accuracy), bold = TRUE,
           color = "white", background = "#1a9641")
Accuracy en clasificación según el valor de p
p Accuracy
1 0.6486
2 0.6300
3 0.6222 
ggplot(resultados_p_clf, aes(x = p, y = Accuracy)) +
  geom_line(color = "darkorange", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "darkorange", size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Accuracy), vjust = -0.8, size = 3.2) +
  labs(title    = "Accuracy en función de p (Minkowski)",
       subtitle = "Modelo KNN de clasificación",
       x = "Valor de p", y = "Accuracy") +
  theme_minimal() +
  expand_limits(y = c(min(resultados_p_clf$Accuracy)*0.97,
                      max(resultados_p_clf$Accuracy)*1.03))

## Mejor valor de p (regresión)    : 1
## Mejor valor de p (clasificación): 1

La métrica resaltada en verde en cada tabla es la que produjo el mejor desempeño. En la mayoría de problemas con variables numéricas escaladas, la distancia de Manhattan (p = 1) o la Euclidiana (p = 2) suelen ser las más eficientes. Las variables predictoras del dataset son numéricas pero de naturaleza heterogénea (conteos discretos y ratios). La distancia de Manhattan trata cada dimensión de forma aditiva e independiente, lo que es coherente con esta heterogeneidad. Valores de p mayores a 2 amplifican las diferencias en la dimensión más extrema, lo que en presencia de ruido empeora la predicción.

Comparación final con árbol de decisión, Random Forest y Naive Bayes

set.seed(123)

t0 <- Sys.time()
modelo_arbol_clf <- rpart(precio_cat ~ . - price,
                          data = train_cat, method = "class",
                          control = rpart.control(xval = 10))
cp_opt_clf <- modelo_arbol_clf$cptable[which.min(
                modelo_arbol_clf$cptable[, "xerror"]), "CP"]
modelo_arbol_clf <- prune(modelo_arbol_clf, cp = cp_opt_clf)
t_arbol <- as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))

pred_arbol_clf <- predict(modelo_arbol_clf, newdata = test_cat, type = "class")
cm_arbol_clf   <- confusionMatrix(pred_arbol_clf, test_cat$precio_cat)

set.seed(123)
idx_rf <- sample(seq_len(nrow(train_cat)), size = min(10000, nrow(train_cat)))
train_rf <- train_cat[idx_rf, ]

t0 <- Sys.time()
modelo_rf <- randomForest(precio_cat ~ . - price,
                          data = train_rf, ntree = 100)
t_rf <- as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))

pred_rf <- predict(modelo_rf, newdata = test_cat)
cm_rf   <- confusionMatrix(pred_rf, test_cat$precio_cat)

t0 <- Sys.time()
modelo_nb_t <- naiveBayes(precio_cat ~ . - price, data = train_cat)
t_nb <- as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))

pred_nb_t <- predict(modelo_nb_t, newdata = test_cat)
cm_nb_t   <- confusionMatrix(pred_nb_t, test_cat$precio_cat)

t0 <- Sys.time()
set.seed(123)
modelo_knn_final <- train(
  precio_cat ~ .,
  data      = train_cat_tune,
  method    = "kknn",
  trControl = trainControl(method = "cv", number = 3),
  tuneGrid  = modelo_knn_tune_clf$bestTune,
  metric    = "Accuracy"
)
t_knn <- as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs"))

pred_knn_final <- predict(modelo_knn_final, newdata = test_cat_clf)
cm_knn_final   <- confusionMatrix(pred_knn_final, test_cat_clf$precio_cat)
extraer_metricas <- function(cm, nombre, tiempo) {
  data.frame(
    Modelo     = nombre,
    Accuracy   = round(cm$overall["Accuracy"], 4),
    Kappa      = round(cm$overall["Kappa"],    4),
    F1_macro   = round(mean(cm$byClass[, "F1"], na.rm = TRUE), 4),
    Sens_media = round(mean(cm$byClass[, "Sensitivity"], na.rm = TRUE), 4),
    Tiempo_seg = round(tiempo, 3)
  )
}

tabla_final_clf <- rbind(
  extraer_metricas(cm_arbol_clf, "Árbol de Decisión", t_arbol),
  extraer_metricas(cm_rf,        "Random Forest",     t_rf),
  extraer_metricas(cm_nb_t,      "Naive Bayes",       t_nb),
  extraer_metricas(cm_knn_final, "KNN Tuneado",       t_knn)
)

mejor_final_idx <- which.max(tabla_final_clf$Accuracy)

knitr::kable(tabla_final_clf,
             col.names = c("Modelo","Accuracy","Kappa","F1 macro",
                           "Sens. media","Tiempo (s)"),
             caption   = "Tabla 7. Comparación final — modelos de clasificación",
             align     = c("l","r","r","r","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(mejor_final_idx, bold = TRUE, color = "white",
           background = "#1a9641")
Tabla 7. Comparación final — modelos de clasificación
Modelo Accuracy Kappa F1 macro Sens. media Tiempo (s)
Accuracy Árbol de Decisión 0.6106 0.4161 0.6001 0.6112 1.703
Accuracy1 Random Forest 0.8568 0.7852 0.8570 0.8569 1.191
Accuracy2 Naive Bayes 0.5648 0.3474 0.5460 0.5654 0.057
Accuracy3 KNN Tuneado 0.6506 0.4759 0.6512 0.6508 4.682 
tabla_long <- tabla_final_clf %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = c(Accuracy, Kappa, F1_macro, Sens_media),
                      names_to = "Metrica", values_to = "Valor")

ggplot(tabla_long, aes(x = Metrica, y = Valor, fill = Modelo)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(Valor, 3)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
            vjust = -0.4, size = 2.8) +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title = "Comparación de métricas — todos los modelos de clasificación",
       x = NULL, y = "Valor", fill = "Modelo") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  expand_limits(y = 1.05)

ggplot(tabla_final_clf, aes(x = reorder(Modelo, Tiempo_seg),
                            y = Tiempo_seg, fill = Modelo)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2fs", Tiempo_seg)),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Set2") +
  labs(title    = "Tiempo de entrenamiento por modelo",
       subtitle = "Menor tiempo = mayor eficiencia computacional",
       x = NULL, y = "Tiempo (segundos)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  expand_limits(y = max(tabla_final_clf$Tiempo_seg) * 1.18)

tiempos <- tabla_final_clf %>% select(Modelo, Tiempo_seg) %>% arrange(Tiempo_seg)

knitr::kable(tiempos,
             col.names = c("Modelo","Tiempo (s)"),
             caption   = "Tiempos de entrenamiento ordenados (más rápido a más lento)",
             align     = c("l","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","condensed"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which.max(tiempos$Tiempo_seg), bold = TRUE, background = "#fee090")
Tiempos de entrenamiento ordenados (más rápido a más lento)
Modelo Tiempo (s)
Accuracy2 Naive Bayes 0.057
Accuracy1 Random Forest 1.191
Accuracy Árbol de Decisión 1.703
Accuracy3 KNN Tuneado 4.682 
## Mejor modelo de clasificación: Random Forest
## Accuracy: 0.8568

De los cuatro algoritmos evaluados, el modelo resaltado en verde en la Tabla 7 es el que clasifica con mayor precisión las categorías de precio sobre el conjunto de prueba.

Random Forest suele destacar porque combina múltiples árboles decorrelacionados que reducen la varianza sin aumentar el sesgo, capturando relaciones no lineales y de interacción. Sin embargo, tiene el mayor costo computacional durante el entrenamiento.

KNN Tuneado ofrece un balance entre interpretabilidad y desempeño. Su limitación principal es el costo de predicción, que escala con el tamaño del conjunto de entrenamiento.

Árbol de Decisión es el más rápido y el más interpretable, pero tiende a mayor varianza; la poda con validación cruzada mitiga el sobreajuste.

Naive Bayes es el modelo más simple y más rápido, pero la asunción de independencia entre variables predictoras lo penaliza cuando las variables están correlacionadas.

La elección del modelo definitivo depende del contexto de negocio: si se prioriza la precisión, Random Forest o KNN tuneado son preferibles; si se prioriza la interpretabilidad y la velocidad, el Árbol de Decisión es una opción sólida.
